analogy: Think of "Social Justice" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 4 Social Justice Overview Just as we intuitively understand what love means even if we cannot explain all its different shades of meaning, we also have an intuitive understanding of justice even though we may not be able to define it precisely. In addition,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 4.1 WHAT IS JUSTICE?, LET’S THINK, 4.2 JUST DISTRIBUTION, 4.3 JOHN RAWLS’ THEORY OF JUSTICE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Social Justice is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 4 Social Justice Overview Just as we intuitively understand what love means even if we cannot explain all its different shades of meaning, we also have an intuitive understanding of justice even though we may not be able to define it precisely.

The text explains that in addition, both love and justice evoke passionate responses from their advocates.

The text explains that and as with love, no one hates justice, everyone wants justice for oneself and to some extent for others also.

The text explains that but unlike love, which is an aspect of our relationships with a few people whom we know well, justice concerns our life in society, the way in which public life is ordered and the principles according to which social goods and social duties are distributed among different members of society.

The text explains that as such, questions of justice are of central importance for politics.

The text explains that after going through this chapter you should be able to: o Identify some of the principles of justice which have been put forward in different societies and at different periods of time. o Explain what is meant by distributive justice. o Discuss John Rawls’ argument that a fair and just society would be in the interest of all members and could be defended on rational grounds.

The text explains that justice Social Justice 54 Political Theory 4.1 WHAT IS JUSTICE?

The text explains that all cultures and traditions have grappled with questions of justice although they may have interpreted the concept in different ways.

The text explains that for instance, in ancient Indian society, justice was associated with dharma and maintaining dharma or a just social order, was considered to be a primary duty of kings.

The text explains that in China, Confucius, the famous philosopher argued that kings should maintain justice by punishing wrong doers and rewarding the virtuous.

The text explains that athens (Greece), Plato discussed issues of justice in his book The Republic.

The text explains that through a long dialogue between Socrates and his young friends, Glaucon and Adeimantus, Plato examined why we should be concerned about justice.

The text explains that the young people ask Socrates why we should be just.

The text explains that they observe that people who were unjust seemed to be much better off than those who were just.

The text explains that those who twisted rules to serve their interests, avoided paying taxes and were willing to lie and be deceitful, were often more successful than those who were truthful and just.

The text explains that if one were smart enough to avoid being caught then it would seem that being unjust is better than being just.

The text explains that he explained that justice does not only mean doing good to our friends and harm to our enemies, or pursuing our own interests.

The text explains that justiceSocial JusticePolitical Theory 55 concerned with the well-being of his/her patients, similarly the just ruler or the just government must be concerned with the well-being of the people.

The text explains that equal Treatment for Equals Although there might be broad agreement in modern society about the equal importance of all people, it is not a simple matter to decide how to give each person his/her due.

The text explains that therefore they deserve equal rights and equal treatment.

The text explains that some of the important rights which are granted in most liberal democracies today include civil rights such as the rights of life, liberty and property, political rights like the right to vote, which enable people to participate in political processes, and certain social rights which would include the right to enjoy equal opportunities with other members of the society.

The text explains that apart from equal rights, the principle of treating equals equally would require that people should not be discriminated against on grounds of class, caste, race or gender.

The text explains that in other words, provided everybody starts from the same base line of equal rights, justice in such cases would mean rewarding people in proportion to the scale and quality of their effort.

The text explains that if we use these criteria we may find that certain kinds of workers in our society are not paid a wage which takes such factors sufficiently into account.

The text explains that for instance, miners, skilled craftsmen, or people in sometimes dangerous but socially useful professions like policemen, may not always get a reward which is just if we compare it to what some others in society may be earning.

The text explains that for justice in society, the principle of equal treatment needs to be balanced with the principle of proportionality.

The text explains that recognition of Special Needs A third principle of justice which we recognise is for a society to take into account special needs of people while distributing rewards or duties.

The text explains that in terms of their basic status and rights as members of the society justice may require that people be treated equally.

The text explains that but even non- discrimination between people and rewarding them proportionately to their efforts might not be enough to ensure that people enjoy equality in other aspects of their lives in society nor that the society

The text explains that it is believed that if people who enjoy very different standard of living and opportunities are treated equally in all respects with those who have been deprived of even the basic minimum needs to live a healthy and productive life, the result is likely to be an unequal society, not an egalitarian and just one.

The text explains that the teacher decided to give her also three hours and thirty minutes to finish her mathematics paper. o A teacher gives grace marks to the weaker students in class, to boost their morale. o A professor distributes different question papers to different students based on her evaluation of their capabilities. o There is a proposal to reserve 33 per cent of the seats in the Parliament for women. as a whole is just.

The text explains that the Constitution therefore allowed for reservations of government jobs and quotas for admissions to educational institutions for people belonging to the Scheduled Castes and Tribes.

The text explains that our discussion of different principles of justice has indicated that governments might sometimes find it difficult to harmonise the three principles of justice which have been discussed — equal treatment for equals, recognition of different efforts and skills while determining rewards and burdens, and provision of minimum standard of living and equal opportunities to the needy.

The text explains that it then becomes a function of governments to harmonise the different principles to promote a just society.

The text explains that 4.2 JUST DISTRIBUTION To achieve social justice in society, governments might have to do more than just ensure that laws and policies treat individuals in a fair manner.

The text explains that justiceSocial JusticePolitical Theory 59 groups and individuals within a society.

The text explains that if there are serious economic or social inequalities in a society, it might become necessary to try and redistribute some of the important resources of the society to provide something like a level playing field for citizens.

The text explains that therefore, within a country social justice would require not only that people be treated equally in terms of the laws and policies of the society but also that they enjoy some basic equality of life conditions and opportunities.

The text explains that in our country for instance, the Constitution abolished the practice of untouchability to promote social equality and ensure that people belonging to ‘lower’ castes have access to temples, jobs and basic necessities like water.

The text explains that different state governments have also taken some measures to redistribute important resources like land in a more fair manner by instituting land reforms.

The text explains that differences of opinion on matters such whether, and how, to distribute resources and ensure equal access to education and jobs arouse fierce passions in society and even sometimes provoke violence.

The text explains that we have only to remind ourselves about the anger and even violence which has sometimes been roused by proposals to reserve seats in educational institutions or in government employment in our country.

The text explains that rawls has argued that there could indeed be a rational justification for acknowledging the need to provide help to the least privileged members of a society.

The text explains that 4.3 JOHN RAWLS’ THEORY OF JUSTICE If people are asked to chose the kind of society in which they would like to live, they are likely to chose one in which the rules and organisation of society allot them a privileged position.

The text explains that justice Social Justice 60 Political Theory the good of society, especially if they believe that their decision is going to have an impact on the kind of life and opportunities their children will have in the future.

The text explains that but such perspectives cannot form the basis of a theory of justice for a society.

The text explains that briefly discuss the three principles of justice outlined in the chapter?

The text explains that does the principle of considering the special needs of people conflict with the principle of equal treatment for all?

The text explains that how does Rawls use the idea of a veil of ignorance to argue that fair and just distribution can be defended on rational grounds?

The text explains that what are generally considered to be the basic minimum requirements of people for living a healthy and productive life?

The text explains that what is the responsibility of governments in trying to ensure this minimum to all?

The text explains that which of the following arguments could be used to justify state action to provide basic minimum conditions of life to all citizens?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 4: Social Justice

prelims_traps: Do not treat 4.1 WHAT IS JUSTICE? and LET’S THINK as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Social Justice; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Social Justice through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 4.1 WHAT IS JUSTICE? and LET’S THINK to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
